coredata.dc:title: Fabricating carbon quantum dots doped ZnIn2S4 nanoflower composites with broad spectrum and enhanced photocatalytic Tetracycline hydrochloride degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Haiqing
Jiang, Yinhua
Yang, Xinyan
Li, Fan
Li, Aiping
Liu, Yan
Zhang, Jianming
Zhou, Zhengzhong
Ni, Liang
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a simple in-situ hydrothermal method was demonstrated for fabricating a novel photocatalyst with broad spectrum response based on carbon quantum dots doped ZnIn2S4 nanoflowers(ZIS/CQDs). Compared with pure ZnIn2S4, ZIS/CQDs samples with broader sunlight spectrum responses and higher photostabilities exhibited enhanced photocatalytic performances towards photodegradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) under UV, visible, NIR light and simulated sunlight irradiation. ZIS/CQDs samples all presented higher NIR light activities while pure ZnIn2S4 showed no such ability. The up-converted photoluminescence property of CQDs in composites played an important role in enhancing NIR light response and photodegradation of TCH. Further, ZIS/CQDs sample also possessed the high visible-light photocatalytic activity to degrade rhodamine B (RhB) and methyl orange (MO). A possible mechanism of CQDs on the enhanced photocatalytic activity of ZIS/CQDs sample was also proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnIn2S4/CQDs
Nanoflowers
Broad spectrum
NIR light photocatalytic
Tetracycline hydrochloride

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the synthesis of ZnIn2S4/CQDs nanohybrids.
The schematic illustration of degradation mechanism by ZnIn2S4/CQDs composite.
XRD patterns of pure ZnIn2S4 and ZIS/CQDs hybrids.
TEM images of ZnIn2S4 (a), CQDs (b) and ZIS/CQDs-2 (c, d) and HRTEM images of ZIS/CQDs-2 (e, f).
Comparisons of FT-IR spectra of ZnIn2S4, ZIS/CQDs samples between 500 and 4000cm−1 (a) and the Raman spectrum of ZIS/CQDs-2 (b).

UV–vis absorption spectra of samples (a), and the inset: the color of pure ZnIn2S4, ZIS/CQDs-1, ZIS/CQDs-2 and ZIS/CQDs-3; Plots of (αhν)1/2 vs. energy (hν) of the samples (b); up-converted photoluminescence spectra of CQDs (c); down-converted photoluminescence spectra of CQDs (d), and the inset: the photograph of the CQDs solution after excitation with a UV lamp (365nm).
Fully survey scanned XPS spectra of ZnIn2S4 and ZIS/CQDs-2 (a); Zn 2p (b), In 3d (c), S 2p (d) and C 1s (e) high-resolution XPS spectra for ZnIn2S4 and ZIS/CQDs-2.

Photolysis of TCH (blank) and photocatalytic activity for TCH degradation over ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4/CQDs composites with different contents of CQDs under visible (a), UV (b), NIR (c) and simulated sunlight irradiation (d).

UV–vis spectrum changes of TCH (a), cycling runs for the photodegradation of TCH (b) and trapping experiment of active species during the photocatalytic degradation of TCH (c) over ZIS/CQDs-2 composite under visible light irradiation.
Photo-luminescence (PL) spectra of the pure ZnIn2S4 and ZIS/CQDs-x composites.
Photocurrent (a) and Nyquist plots (b) for ZnIn2S4 and ZIS/CQDs-2 under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Fabricating carbon quantum dots doped ZnIn2S4 nanoflower composites with broad spectrum and enhanced photocatalytic Tetracycline hydrochloride degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Rock Salt and Concave Soil Resources Deep Utilization, Key Laboratory for Palygorskite Science and Applied Technology of Jiangsu Province, Huaiyin Institute of Technology, Huaian, 223003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Rock Salt and Concave Soil Resources Deep Utilization
Key Laboratory for Palygorskite Science and Applied Technology of Jiangsu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Key Laboratory of Mineral Materials and Application, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Key Laboratory of Mineral Materials and Application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a simple in-situ hydrothermal method was demonstrated for fabricating a novel photocatalyst with broad spectrum response based on carbon quantum dots doped ZnIn2S4 nanoflowers(ZIS/CQDs). Compared with pure ZnIn2S4, ZIS/CQDs samples with broader sunlight spectrum responses and higher photostabilities exhibited enhanced photocatalytic performances towards photodegradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) under UV, visible, NIR light and simulated sunlight irradiation. ZIS/CQDs samples all presented higher NIR light activities while pure ZnIn2S4 showed no such ability. The up-converted photoluminescence property of CQDs in composites played an important role in enhancing NIR light response and photodegradation of TCH. Further, ZIS/CQDs sample also possessed the high visible-light photocatalytic activity to degrade rhodamine B (RhB) and methyl orange (MO). A possible mechanism of CQDs on the enhanced photocatalytic activity of ZIS/CQDs sample was also proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: Sparked by the deficiency of global energy supply and growing environmental issues, an enormous challenge aiming at a better utilization of solar light energy and high conversion of solar energy is the extremely urgent over the past decades [1,2]. Photocatalytic technology as a “green” approach is a compelling strategy for environmental management due to its relatively low cost and high efficiency with the precious non-secondary pollution feature [3,4]. Since the first photocatalytic water splitting was reported under ultraviolet (UV) light [5], considerable attentions have been devoted to designing semiconductor photocatalysts for the efficient application and conversion of solar energy. However, restricted to the low solar energy utilization efficiency and the serious recombination of the photogenerated charges, the photocatalytic performances of many semiconductors are limited. It is therefore highly desirable to compensate for these drawbacks, and to seek novel photocatalytic materials with well-defined morphology, favorable recyclability, high stability and robust for efficiently harvesting solar energy and high separation efficiency of photogenerated charges.

In recent years, metal sulfides have demonstrated promising performances for potential application due to the relatively higher VB position and strong visible-light absorption property [6]. Among all the ternary metal sulfides, the chalcogenide ZnIn2S4 has a suitable band gap corresponding to the visible light region. Previous studies have suggested that the visible light-active ZnIn2S4 has significant advantages, such as considerable chemical stability, low toxicity and facile preparation method [7,8]. However, ZnIn2S4 suffers from the quick recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and low migration ability of the photoexcited charge carriers, which in fact are the essential issues for most of semiconductors [9]. Naturally, to optimize the photocatalytic activity of ZnIn2S4, many efforts have been focused on enhancing light absorption and eliminating the recombination centers of photoinduced electrons and holes of ZnIn2S4 by a variety of strategies, such as doping with metals [1,10,11], controlling the shape, size, and structure [12,13], incorporation of RGO, g-C3N4 and other carbon materials [7,9,14–16], and coupling with other semiconductor materials [17–19]. However, the above-mentioned visible-active photocatalysts is still limited in visible-light range and only less than 50% of solar light can be used in their photocatalytic systems. So it remains the most challenging issue to explore broad-spectrum responsive photocatalysts to harvest solar light from ultraviolet (UV) to near-infrared (NIR) wavelength [20].

Until now, only few semiconductors possess NIR photocatalytic activity, such as Bi2WO6 [21], Cu2(OH)PO4 [22]. Unfortunately, these narrow band gap semiconductors with NIR light adsorption generally lack photocatalytic activity due to the low photon energy and the strong thermoeffect [23]. Thus, in order to improve the solar energy utilization, great efforts focused on converting NIR light (accounting for 48%) into visible and UV light by using up-conversion materials. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new member of nanostructured carbon material, possess high aqueous solubility, high biocompatibility, low toxicity, interesting fluorescent and unique photogenerated electron transfer and accepted properties due to the conjugated π structure of CQDs [24–27]. Especially, carbon quantum dots have both up- and down-conversion abilities to serve as spectral converter for efficiently utilizing the full spectrum of sunlight [24]. Recently, the CQDs-based hybrids become promising agents for solar energy application and conversion as visible-light-driven and broad spectrum active photocatalysts, such as CQDs/Bi2WO6 [28], BiVO4/CQDs [29], TiO2/CQDs [30], BiOX(X=Br, Cl)/CQDs [31], TaON/CQDs [32], Pt/CQDs-ZnIn2S4 [33], and CQDs/Ag/Ag2O [34], etc. More significantly, the integration of CQDs with above-mentioned photocatalysts has demonstrated the increase of their visible-light absorption region and the inhibition of photoinduced electron-hole recombination simultaneously, so the activities of composite photocatalysts have been enhanced. However, there are still grand challenges to develop more stable, higher photocatalytic efficient and broader spectrum responsive CQDs-related materials.

Considering the remarkable properties of CQDs and ZnIn2S4, the combination up- and down-conversion and good electron transfer by carbon quantum dots with suitable band gap for ZnIn2S4 will offer an ideal UV-vis-NIR-driven and high efficient photocatalyst in the photocatalysis systems. Herein, we develop a novel broad spectrum responsive photocatalyst based on carbon quantum dots doped ZnIn2S4 nanoflowers(ZIS/CQDs) with monodispersed carbon quantum dots uniformly embedded into the surfaces of ZnIn2S4 nanoflowers to form intimate contacts. The obtained ZIS/CQDs composite photocatalysts were characterized, and their photocatalytic activities were evaluated for tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) degradation under UV, visible, simulated sunlight and NIR light irradiation. The CQDs doped ZnIn2S4 nanoflowers exhibited higher photocatalytic property in comparison with pure ZnIn2S4 under wide spectrum (UV, visible, simulated sunlight and NIR light) irradiation, indicating that CQDs is an excellent enhancer to improve the photocatalytic activity of ZnIn2S4 for photocatalytic degradation. Furthermore, a possible enhanced mechanism of CQDs on the photocatalytic activity of ZIS/CQDs nanohybrids is also proposed. The structural design and functionality enhancement of this new type of broad spectrum photocatalytic material are conducive to the practical utilization of solar energy.

The carbon quantum dots were synthesized according to the reported methods [35]. In a typical procedure, 1.051g citric acid and 335μL ethylenediamine were added to 10mL deionized (DI) water. The mixture was then transferred into a 50mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept at 200°C for 5h in an electric oven. Finally, after cooling to room temperature, the brown-black product was collected and then subjected to dialysis for two days followed by the freeze-drying. The solid CQDs powders were dispersed into water to prepare a suspension (3.48mg/mL), and the suspension was stored at room temperature for further application.

In the in-situ hydrothermal synthesis route, the precursors of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (0.58mmol, 0.1725g), In(NO3)3·4.5H2O (1.0mmol, 0.3820g), thioacetamide (3mmol, 0.2250g) and hexadecylthyrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, 0.1500mg) were dissolved in 35mL deionized (DI) water with different volume (1mL, 3mL and 5mL) of the CQDs suspension. After being vigorously stirred for 30min at room temperature, the contents were transferred into a 45mL Teflon-lined autoclave, which was sealed and heated at 160°C for 6h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the obtained products were collected by centrifugation, washed by deionized water and ethanol for several times, and dried at 60°C for 6h in a vacuum drying oven. Finally, the CQDs doped ZnIn2S4 nanoflowers were obtained and denoted as ZIS/CQDs-x (x=1, 2 and 3), where x represents the adding CQDs amounts varying from 3.48, 10.45 to 17.40mg. The above process is shown in Scheme 1 . For comparison purpose, the ZnIn2S4 was prepared under the identical condition without the addition of CQDs.

The crystal structures of the synthesized samples were recorded by a D/MAX-2500 X-ray diffractometer (Cu-Kα source, λ=1.54178) with the 2θ range from 10 to 80° at a scan rate of 7° min−1. The surface morphologies of the as-prepared samples were observed by S-4800 field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Hitachi High-Technologies, Japan) with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2010, Japan) at 200kV. The high-resolution TEM was performed on field-emission transmission electron microscope (FETEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was measured by a Nicolet-560 FT-IR Spectrometric Analyzer with KBr discs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were done on a Thermo ESCALAB 250× (America) electron spectrometer with 150W Al Kα radiation. The optical property was recorded by UV–vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy (DRS) with a UV-2450 (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan) spectrophotometer using BaSO4 as the background. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained with a Perkin-Elmer LS 55 and all the spectra of PL were taken at room temperature with an excitation wavelength 400nm. The photocurrent measurements were carried out by a CHI 660B (Chenhua, China).

To investigate the photocatalytic activities of the ZnIn2S4 and ZIS/CQDs-x hybrid nanoflowers, the photodegradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH), rhodamine B (RhB) and methyl orange (MO) as target pollutants was carried out at room temperature. All photocatalytic tests of as-prepared samples were done in a GHX-3 photochemical reaction instrument. The visible light was provided by 250W Xe-lamp with a 420nm cutoff filter, a 350W mercury lamp with a maximum emission at 356nm was used as the UV source for photocatalysis, a 150W infrared lamp used as the (N)IR light source and the simulated sunlight source was the 250W Xe-lamp without the cutoff filter. 20mg of photocatalyst was dispersed in 80mL (10mg/L TCH, 20mg/L MO and 20mg/L RhB) aqueous solution. Before illumination, the suspension was constantly stirred for 40min in the dark to ensure the establishment of adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the photocatalyst and the pollutant. During the photocatalytic experiment, the suspension was exposed to the visible light irradiation under magnetic stirring and surrounded by a circulating water bath for cooling. At regular intervals, 3mL of suspension was immediately centrifuged to remove the catalyst, then analyzed by TU-1810 UV–vis spectrometer. Recycling experiments on the degradation of TCH by ZIS/CQDs-2 were carried out under the same experimental conditions. After each cycle, the sample was collected by centrifugation, washed with DI water and absolute ethanol, and then dried at 60°C for 6h at a vacuum drying oven for the next cycle. In order to analysis the degradation of TCH, C/C0 versus time was applied to reflect the degradation efficiency of TCH, wherein C was the TCH concentration at a particular instance, and C0 was the initial TCH concentration.

The detection experiments of photogenerated radicals and holes (OH, O2− and h+) were operated by adding isopropanol (IPA), 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) and triethanolamine (TEOA), respectively. The scavengers of IPA, TEOA and BQ are 1mmol, 1mmol and 0.1mmol in the trapping experiments, respectively. And the photodegradation condition was the same as the above photocatalytic tests.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was carried out to characterize the crystal structure of sample ZnIn2S4 and as-prepared ZIS/CQDs-x nanohybrids. As shown in Fig. 1 , all the diffraction peaks at 2θ=20.47°, 28.52°, 47.50°, 56.23° and 76.78° could be well indexed to (006), (102), (110), (022) and (213) lattice plane of hexagonal phase of ZnIn2S4 (JCPDS card No. 03-065-2023) [36]. In the patterns of ZIS/CQDs-x nanocomposites, all the diffraction peaks can be indexed to the known hexagonal ZnIn2S4 indicating that carbon quantum dots did not affect the crystal phase of ZnIn2S4. Besides, no diffraction peaks belonging to impurity can be observed in ZIS/CQDs-x samples, implying the high phase purity of resultant products. From Fig. 1, no diffractions peaks corresponding to CQDs have been observed in the XRD patterns of the ZIS/CQDs-x products due to its low amount and its high dispersion on the surface of ZnIn2S4.

The TEM images of as-synthesized ZnIn2S4, CQDs and ZIS/CQDs-2 are shown in Fig. 2 . The TEM image of ZnIn2S4 (Fig. 2a) shows that the regular ZnIn2S4 nanoflower assembled by a lot of nanosheets has been synthesized. The size of ZnIn2S4 nanoflowers is about several hundred nanometers. From Fig. 2b, the uniform distribution and non-aggregation of the monodispersed CQDs nanoparticles can be clearly observed, and the particle sizes of CQDs fall in the range 2–4nm. The TEM image of ZIS/CQDs-2 is shown in Fig. 2c. Compared to the ZnIn2S4, the spherical structure of ZIS/CQDs-2 nanoflower becomes somewhat less regular and smaller, which indicates that the addition of CQDs might influence the growth of ZnIn2S4 nanoflowers. Moreover, a zoomed fraction of the edge of a nanoflower (Fig. 2d) reveals the carbon quantum dots with spherical particles are uniformly embedded into the surfaces of ZnIn2S4, indicating that the CQDs doped ZnIn2S4 nanohybrids have been synthesized. For the clear observation, the high-resolution TEM images in Fig. 2 e and f show the lattice spacing of 0.324nm and 0.41nm assigned to (102) and (006) planes of the hexagonal ZnIn2S4 phase. The inter-planar spacing of around 0.320nm corresponds to (002) crystallographic planes of CQDs in the composite, fully demonstrating that CQDs has been introduced to the ZnIn2S4 phase and there exists the imitate contact between CQDs and ZnIn2S4 in CQDs-ZIS nanoybrids. The EDS pattern (Fig. S1) shows the sample ZIS/CQDs-2 contains C, Zn, In and S element, which proves that the ZIS/CQDs-2 composite is prepared successfully.

In order to determine the presence of CQDs, FT-IR and Raman spectra were characterized. Fig. 3 a shows the FT-IR spectra of CQDs, ZnIn2S4 and ZIS/CQDs-x composites. For the FT-IR spectra of CQDs, it is obviously observed the peaks at about 3408 and 1374cm−1 indicate the presence of hydroxyl groups on the CQDs. The peak at 2958cm−1 is the CH stretch of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. The peaks at approximately 1650 and 1558cm−1 are corresponding to the vibrational absorption band of CO and bending vibrations of NH groups, respectively [37]. As for pristine ZnIn2S4, the peaks at around 1385 and 3408cm−1 are corresponding to the surface absorbed water molecules and hydroxyl groups. The peaks at about 2852 and 2922cm−1 are the stretch vibrations of CH from the little residual CTAB. However, the characteristic peaks of CQDs have not been observed in the ZIS/CQDs-x composites because of the low content. Therefore, Raman spectrum was used to provide further structural insight of as-prepared ZIS/CQDs-2 composite. As shown in Fig. 3b, two characteristic bands located at about 1338.11 and 1601.33cm−1 are observed in the ZIS/CQDs-2 composite, which are corresponded to the D-band (the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite or glassy carbon) and the G-band (the in-plane bond stretching motion of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice), respectively [38]. Thus, the above mentioned results have confirmed the formation of ZIS/CQDs-2 composite.

The optical properties of the samples are essential to determine their photocatalytic performance. The optical absorption characteristics of ZnIn2S4, ZIS/CQDs-x samples were measured by obtaining UV–vis absorption spectra in the wavelength range of 200–800nm, as shown in Fig. 4 a. Obviously, the shapes of absorption spectra for all the samples are almost the same except the absorption band-edge. The absorption band-edge of pristine ZnIn2S4 has occurred at 545nm. In comparison of ZnIn2S4, after the introduction of CQDs, the absorption edges of ZIS/CQDs-1 (548nm), ZIS/CQDs-2 (562nm) and ZIS/CQDs-3 (577nm) are red-shift slightly, which can be ascribed to the light absorption of CQDs, the light scattering of fractal rough surface from ZnIn2S4 nanoflowers [33] and the beneficial interaction between CQDs and ZnIn2S4. In addition, as the content of CQDs increases, the absorption intensities of composite samples in visible light region increases, which also verify the color alteration from yellow to brownish yellow (the inset in Fig. 4a). Together with the intrinsic merits of the floriated nanosphere and the CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanoflowers, this composite system has efficiently promoted broad spectrum absorption and presents its great potential for sunlight photocatalytic degradation.

Fig. 4b shows the estimated optical band gaps (Eg) of as-prepared samples by plotting (αhν)1/2 with respect to hν . According to equation [39]:
(1)
(αhν)1/2
=
A(hν −Eg)
where α is the absorption coefficient, hν is the photo energy, Eg is the band gap and A is a constant. Apparently, the tangent of ZnIn2S4 curve extrapolates to the photon energy axis, and the band gap energy is 2.15eV, which is inconsistent with the reported work due to the dependence of band gap on the particle size and morphology. Because of the red-shift of region edge, the band gaps of as-prepared composites have been narrowed. The calculated band gaps of ZIS/CQDs-1, ZIS/CQDs-2 and ZIS/CQDs-3 were 2.07, 1.99 and 1.90eV, respectively.

In order to explain more details for promising superiority of CQDs in broaden spectrum response of photocatalyst, the up-converted and down-converted photoluminescence spectra of CQDs have been conducted. As illustrated in Fig. 4c, it can be observed that the emissions of PL spectra of CQDs excited by long-wavelength (NIR) light from 1000 to 700nm located at visible light region in the wavelength range of 470–550nm. The result shows CQDs present the up-conversion ability to transfer NIR light to visible light, implying the photocatalyst can take full usage of visible-NIR light to form electron-hole pairs, which can in turn strengthen the photocatalytic activities [40]. Fig. 4d shows the PL spectra of CQDs solution within the excitation range of 320–400nm. The emission peaks are located at visible light region in the wavelength range of 430–500nm, which proves that the CQDs have down-conversion property. The inset in Fig. 4d shows the bright blue PL of dispersed CQDs under UV light (365nm), which can be easily seen with the naked eye. From the above results of CQDs PL spectra, the CQD-based photocatalyst can make full use of solar light.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to characterize the chemical compositions and surface properties of elements in the ZnIn2S4 and ZIS/CQDs-2. The full survey scanned spectra in Fig. 5 a exhibit the presence of Zn 2p, In 3d, S 2p and C 1s in the ZnIn2S4 and ZIS/CQDs-2 composite. From the high-resolution XPS spectra of Zn 2p in ZnIn2S4 (Fig. 5b), and two peaks fitted at 1020.68 and 1043.88eV correspond to the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 of ZnIn2S4, which is consistent with the spectrum of Zn2+. In Fig. 5c, two peaks fitted at 443.88 and 451.68eV are ascribed to the 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 levels of In3+ in ZnIn2S4. Moreover, the peaks for S 2p in ZnIn2S4 at 159.48 and 162.28eV illustrated in Fig. 5d, which can be attributed to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of ZnIn2S4, respectively. However, compared with high-resolution XPS spectra of ZnIn2S4, the positions of the different peaks of the Zn 2p and In 3d in ZIS/CQDs-2 have shifted to a lower binding energies, while S 2p was shifted to a higher binding energy. These shifts could be attributed to the introduction of CQDs and a strong electronic coupling between ZnIn2S4 and CQDs [2]. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 5e, after curve fitting, the three characteristic peaks of C 1s for ZIS/CQDs-2 composite at 285, 286.5 and 288.9eV are assigned to sp2-hybridized carbon (e.g. CC and CC bonds), CO bonds and OCO bond, respectively [41]. The high resolution XPS spectrum of ZIS/CQDs-2 of C 1s further confirms the coexistence of ZnIn2S4 and CQDs in the ZIS/CQDs hybrid system.

Given the excellent optical properties, the photocatalytic performance of the ZnIn2S and as-prepared composite samples ZIS/CQDs-x were evaluated for the degradation of tetracycline (TCH) solution under visible light, UV, simulated sunlight and NIR light irradiation. Before the irradiation, the adsorption-desorption equilibrium experiment was done in the dark for 40min. And it is noted that the absorption-desorption balance between the photocatalyst and TCH has reached within 20min in the dark (Fig. 6 ) and the adsorption efficiencies of as-prepared samples are all lower than 20%. As shown in Fig. 6a, without catalysts, only 1.49% TCH was decomposed, indicating that TCH photolysis has made little difference on the photocatalysis. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of TCH over pure ZnIn2S4 sample is only 54.48% under visible light irradiation for 90min. However, the degradation efficiencies of ZIS/CQDs-x exhibit excellent enhancement compared to pristine ZnIn2S4. Especially, the visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation activity of ZIS/CQDs-2 nanoflowers is superior to those of ZnIn2S4 (58.84%), ZIS/CQDs-1 (64.79%) and ZIS/CQDs-3 (67.89%), with the best degradation rate being 85.07%. In addition, the kinetics of degradation was also used to investigate the photodegradation efficiency of as-obtained samples. By plotting ln(C0/C) versus time, as shown in Fig. S2a, the photodegradation of TCH over ZIS/CQDs-x should follow the first order due to the straight lines and the rate constants were determined from these lines. The rate constants of pristine ZnIn2S4, ZIS/CQDs-1, ZIS/CQDs-2 and ZIS/CQDs-3 are 0.00685, 0.00758, 0.01107 and 0.00839min−1, respectively (Fig. S2b). Obviously, the ZIS/CQDs-2 shows the highest rate constant. Fig. 6b shows the results of TCH degradation efficiencies by ZnIn2S4 and as-prepared composite samples ZIS/CQDs-x under UV light irradiation. As shown, the efficiencies of ZIS/CQDs-x are all superior to that of ZnIn2S4, and the photocatalytic of TCH over ZIS/CQDs-2 is over 84% (30min), which shows that the ZIS/CQDs-x are good UV-light-driven photocatalysts. Fig. 6c indicates the results of TCH NIR light degradation efficiencies by ZnIn2S4 and ZIS/CQDs-x samples. The concentration of TCH by use of pure ZnIn2S4 as photocatalyst has been decreased 15.21% due to the absorption because the floriated architectures with high surface area could absorb the pollutants molecules. After the NIR light irradiation, the TCH degradation almost can be omitted for pure ZnIn2S4 because pure ZnIn2S4 cannot absorb NIR light. However, all ZIS/CQDs-x composites show higher NIR light TCH degradation efficiencies than ZnIn2S4, and the ZIS/CQDs-2 presented the highest photocatalytic property for TCH degradation, about 43.85% of TCH degraded within 4h. The up-converted photoluminescence property of CQDs in composites played an important role in enhancing the NIR light response and resulted in the higher NIR light photodegradation of TCH. Under the simulated sunlight irradiation, ZIS/CQDs-x composites exhibit superior photocatalytic activities, and sample ZIS/CQDs-2 also possesses the highest photocatalytic activity for TCH (87%) degradation (Fig. 6d). Based on above-mentioned results, it can be found that the photocatalytic degradation performance of the ZIS/CQDs-2 is higher than those of pure ZnIn2S4 and ZIS/CQDs-x (x=1 and 3). Thus, the improved visible, UV, simulated sunlight and NIR light photocatalytic property of the ZIS/CQDs-2 can be attributed to the high surface area and the strong light harvesting from floriated ZnIn2S4 nanospheres, electron reservoir from CQDs, up- and down-converted photoluminescence properties of CQDs and reduced charge transfer resistance. Especially, the NIR photocatalytic property of composite ZIS/CQDs greatly improved, so the hybrid of CQDs and ZnIn2S4 can efficiently broaden solar light absorption region and improve electron-hole pairs’ separation as a result for the higher photocatalytic activity. These results efficiently prove that the ZIS/CQDs composite can be applied as a good UV–vis-NIR sunlight-driven photocatalyst for the degradation of organic pollutants.

More interestingly, the addition content of CQDs in this composite system has a significant influence on the photocatalytic performances of ZIS/CQDs-x photocatalysts. With increase of CQDs content in ZIS/CQDs-x system, the photocatalytic activities of ZIS/CQDs-x composites were firstly enhanced, and the ZIS/CQDs-2 photocatalyst exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity. Nevertheless, with further increasing in the CQDs content, the ZIS/CQDs-3 shows a decreasing photocatalytic activity, which could be attributed to the decreasing conductivity of photocatalyst for electron transport and the shadowing effect due to excessive CQDs content. To further demonstrate the enhanced photocatalytic performance of the ZIS/CQDs-x, the photocatalytic degradations of RhB and MO were conducted under visible light irradiation, as shown in Fig. S3 and S4 . The ZIS/CQDs-2 exhibited the highest photocatalytic activities (99% for RhB in 60min and 91% for MO in 240min) in all ZIS/CQDs-x samples for the decoration of moderate amounts of CQDs.

The characteristic absorption peaks of TCH solution degraded by sample ZIS/CQDs-2 at different time are illustrated in Fig. 7 a. The absorbance at 357nm gradually decreases as the time increased and almost disappeared in 90min, which reveals that TCH was gradually degraded. Meanwhile, the absorption peaks at 276 and 353nm decrease and show a red shift, indicating that some of the TCH is mineralized to small molecules such as CO2 and H2O that could absorb UV light [42].

To investigate the stability of the prepared samples, four consecutive test cycles of photocatalytic degradation TCH were carried out using the ZIS/CQDs-2 as a representative nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 7b, it can be observed over 4 cycles that ZIS/CQDs-2 shows a consistent photocatalytic degradation rate with little diminishing photocatalytic activities under identical experimental conditions, implying that the as-prepared photocatalyst has a high durability regarding the prolonged reaction duration.

The trapping experiment of active species was conducted via adding different scavengers to give more details about the mechanism of degradation by the ZIS/CQDs-2 composite. As shown in Fig. 7c, when the isopropyl alcohol (IPA) as trapping hydroxyl radicals (OH) scavenger was added into reaction solution, the photocatalytic efficiency is nearly unchanged. However, once the triethanolamine (TEOA, holes scavenger) or benzoquinone (BQ, superoxide radicals (O2 −) scavenger) was added to the photocatalytic process, the degradation efficiencies of TCH are remarkably reduced (67.8% for TEOA, 48.2% for BQ), which mean that holes and superoxide radicals are the most active species during the photocatalytic degradation process. Moreover, the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) potentials of ZnIn2S4 were calculated by using an empirical equation. The VB potentials of ZnIn2S4 was determined using the EVB =X- Ee +0.5Eg, where EVB is the VB potentials, Eg is the band gap of the semiconductor, X is the geometric mean of the absolute electronegativity of the constituent atoms in the semiconductor and Ee is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (4.5eV vs. NHE) [43]. The X values for ZnIn2S4 is 4.86eV and Eg is 1.99eV calculated according to DRS analysis [19]. In addition, the CB potential can be acquired from the formula ECB =EVB − Eg. The calculated values for EVB and ECB of ZnIn2S4 are 1.355eV and −0.635eV, respectively.

To investigate the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in photocatalysts, photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of ZnIn2S4 and ZIS/CQDs-x composites were conducted to characterize the recombination of free charge carriers [44]. As depicted in Fig. 8 , the excitation wavelength is determined at 400nm, and the PL spectrum of pristine ZnIn2S4 shows a strong emission peak around 529nm, which is the main peak attributed to the emission of band gap transition of ZnIn2S4. As for ZIS/CQDs-x samples, the intensities of emission bands decreased significantly, and sample ZIS/CQDs-2 presents the lowest PL emission peak, suggesting the recombination of photoexcited carriers is effectively prevented. To figure out better the electron-hole pair separation in ZIS/CQDs-2, the analyses of photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were carried out. Fig. 9 a shows the periodic on/off photocurrent responses of ZnIn2S4 and ZIS/CQDs-2 under simulated visible light irradiation. It is obvious that the loading of the CQDs into the surface of ZnIn2S4 nanoflowers can significantly enhance the photocurrent density, which is about 2 times higher than that of ZnIn2S4. Furthermore, Fig. 9b shows the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of obtained ZnIn2S4 and ZIS/CQDs-2. The diameter of semicircle in the medium-frequency region was attributed to the electron transfer resistance controlling the kinetics at the electrode interface in the charge-transfer limiting process [45,46]. The semicircle diameter for the ZIS/CQDs-2 electrode is much smaller than that of ZnIn2S4 due to the large impedance of the ZnIn2S4 electrode. Both the photocurrent and EIS analyses indicate that the introduction of the CQDs significantly reduced the resistance on the movement of charge carriers due to the fast transport of charge carriers. That is to say, the ZIS/CQDs-2 composite possesses higher photocatalytic activity because of the improved separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers in composite system.

Based on above-clarified analysis and results, the enhanced photocatalytic mechanism, as shown in Scheme 2 , is proposed for the role of CQDs in the superior photocatalytic activity of ZIS/CQDs-x system during the photocatalytic process under UV, visible, NIR light irradiation and simulated sunlight irradiation. When the ZnIn2S4 irradiated by UV and visible light, the electrons can be excited from the VB to the CB and the holes were left on the VB. In general, only a portion of photoinduced electrons and holes could participate in the photocatalytic process rather than quickly recombine. After the doping of CQDs, the excited electrons could spatial transfer to the CQDs quickly and achieve efficient charges separation. The photogenerated electrons on the CQDs could react with O2 to produce more O2 −, which is attributed to the CB potential of ZnIn2S4 (-0.635eV) is more negative than the standard redox potential of O2/O2 − (−0.046eV vs. NHE). The O2 − and holes could react with the TCH, MO or RhB molecule to produce decomposed products, where holes can be directly reacted with organic matter. During the photocatalytic process under full spectrum irradiation, the CQDs play an important role in the photodegradation process, resulting in excellent photocatalytic activity. On the one hand, the CQDs can act as collector and transporter of electron to trap and transfer electrons generated on ZnIn2S4 due to UV–vis-light irradiation, hence suppressing the recombination of photoinduced charge, and then enhancing the separation of electron-hole pairs and photocatalytic activity in the ZIS/CQDs-x composites. On the other hand, the CQDs can absorb NIR wavelength light and then emit shorter wavelength light due to its up-converted mechanism, which can further excite ZnIn2S4 to produce more electron-hole pairs. Consequently, ZIS/CQDs-2 can effectively utilize the broad spectrum sunlight to improve the photocatalytic properties of composite sample. Therefore, the practically photocatalytic applications can be enhanced by good sunlight absorption capacity, designing fast charge-transfer channels and efficient electron-hole pair separation via the decoration of the CQDs.

In conclusion, the novel UV–vis-NIR light driven carbon quantum dots doped ZnIn2S4 hybrid nanolfowers are successfully prepared via a simple and efficient in-situ hydrothermal method. The green and monodispersed CQDs were evenly embedded into the nanosheets which form the flower-like ZnIn2S4. The as-obtained ZnIn2S4/carbon quantum dots nanohybrids with broad sunlight spectrum absorption capacity exhibit excellent UV–vis-NIR light photocatalytic activities for TCH, MO and RhB degradation in aqueous solution and good photocatalytic stabilities. The enhanced UV–vis-NIR photocatalytic properties can be attributed to up-converted photoluminescence property, the extended optical absorption and efficient charge separation and transfer by the addition of CQDs. Furthermore, it is found that ZIS/CQDs-2 nanocomposite presents the most efficient photocatalytic activity for photodecomposition organic contaminants compared with other samples under UV, visible, simulated sunlight and near-infrared light. This work can provide an alternative strategy for designing and developing a new kind of UV–vis-NIR photocatalyst with the efficient utilization of broad-spectrum sunlight and high photocatalytic activity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was funded by the National Basic Research Program of China (21576214 and 21777062), Research Foundation for Advanced Talents of Jiangsu University (16JDG022), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20160495), The Opening Project of Key Laboratory for Palygorskite Science and Applied Technology of Jiangsu Province (HPK201605), the Opening Project of Hunan Key Laboratory of Mineral Materials and Application (MMA201704) and Huaian Science and Technology project (HAS2015002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.materresbull.2017.09.004.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
